title: Bundle and license-vet fonts for both packs
short_description: Select, license-vet, and bundle the typography candidates for both launch style packs as package data (never system fonts), with an attribution file shipped in-package.
long_description_md:
# Bundle and license-vet fonts for both packs

## Why this task exists

This is the last task of **Phase 0: Foundations & determinism rig** (`docs/cofounder/ROADMAP.md`), whose
`Done when` list closes with: "Fonts bundled (both packs' typography candidates, licenses vetted,
attribution file)." It depends on `repo-scaffold-ci` because the fonts ship as package data inside
the `src/anima` layout and wheel-packaging config that task creates — there is nowhere to bundle
fonts into until the package skeleton, `pyproject.toml`, and dev-tooling exist.

The determinism rig this phase builds is only honest if text rendering is deterministic too.
`docs/cofounder/specs/feasibility-report.md` and `technical-architecture.md` both cite the resvg
lesson directly: "bit-exact cross-platform determinism is achievable only by owning the whole
stack — bundle fonts, avoid system shaping/raster libraries. Matplotlib never achieved bit-exact
text in 20 years and engineered around it with tolerances and text removal." The founding
technical decision (`technical-architecture.md` § Key technical decisions #3, and the
2026-07-13 DECISIONS.md entry locking the stack) is explicit: "we bundle fonts and pin
rasterizer." If an agent's render depends on whatever fonts happen to be installed on its host
OS, two things break at once: (1) the same project can render with different glyph shapes/metrics
on different machines — poisoning the golden-image harness this phase also builds — and (2) the
verifier's geometry resolver (which will compute text bounding boxes via the same Skia/FreeType
path the renderer uses, once Phase 1 lands) would be answering a question with a machine-dependent
answer, which contradicts "verified scene graph ⇒ trusted output." This task exists to make "never
system fonts" true from day one, before any code path could grow a dependency on one.

Licensing is the other half. `non-functional-requirements.md` § Compliance states the project's one
real legal obligation: "all bundled fonts/assets/map data carry permissive licenses with attribution
files shipped in-package ... fonts vetted per-asset before bundling." anima-ai is Apache-2.0,
zero-budget, and redistributed by every downstream `pip install` — a font with an unclear or
non-redistributable license would be a supply-chain-shaped legal defect baked into every install,
and is exactly the kind of thing the persona's "nothing gets stubbed" rule forbids skipping.

## What this task builds

1. **Typography research and selection, per pack, grounded in the taste brief.**
   `docs/cofounder/specs/product-spec.md` capability 5 and `ux-design-spec.md` bullet 4 define the
   two poles typography must serve:
   - **`crude`** (OverSimplified-inspired): "confident scrappiness — wobbly linework, blobby
     characters, punchy label pops, comedic timing." Typography needs an informal,
     hand-lettered/marker feel for display/label text and a legible, still-informal companion for
     body/subtitle text. Real, well-known open-license candidates that fit this brief: **Permanent
     Marker** (Apache-2.0, Google Fonts) for display/labels, **Patrick Hand** (SIL OFL-1.1, Google
     Fonts) or **Comic Neue** (SIL OFL-1.1) for body/subtitles. These are starting candidates for
     the research pass, not a mandate — the research engine confirms actual license text and fit
     before locking the selection, and may substitute an equally-fitting permissively-licensed
     alternative.
   - **`flat`** (Kurzgesagt-inspired): "calm precision — geometric shapes, generous whitespace,
     smooth easing." Typography needs a clean geometric sans for display/headings and a companion
     optimized for small-size legibility (subtitles, map labels) for body text. Real open-license
     candidates: **Montserrat** (SIL OFL-1.1, Google Fonts) for display, **Nunito Sans** or
     **Mulish** (SIL OFL-1.1) for body. Same caveat: candidates to evaluate and confirm, not a
     forced final answer.
   - Minimum bar: each pack ships at least two font **roles** — `display` (titles, MapLabel pops,
     InvasionArrow labels) and `body` (subtitles/narration overlays, longer on-screen text) —
     because `data-model.md` already types `Text` nodes with a `font role` field and
     `product-spec.md` capability 2 says subtitles "render the narration text with style-pack
     typography." Additional roles (e.g. a numeric/monospace role for on-map date labels) may be
     added later in Phase 3/5 without revisiting the licensing infrastructure this task builds.
2. **License vetting against a permissive allow-list.** Every candidate font's actual license is
   checked (not assumed from reputation) and only bundled if its SPDX identifier is in
   `{OFL-1.1, Apache-2.0, MIT, CC0-1.0, Unlicense}` — the same permissive-license posture as the
   project's own Apache-2.0 choice, and free of any redistribution, sale, or attribution-format
   restriction that would complicate `pip install anima-ai` shipping the binary font files inside
   the wheel.
3. **Font files bundled as package data**, at `src/anima/assets/fonts/crude/` and
   `src/anima/assets/fonts/flat/` (actual `.ttf`/`.otf` binaries, source-verified from each
   foundry's official distribution), wired into `pyproject.toml`'s package-data configuration
   (whichever build backend `repo-scaffold-ci` selected) so `python -m build --wheel` produces a
   wheel that actually contains the font files — this is the literal difference between "bundled"
   and "referenced." No code anywhere in `src/anima` may resolve a font by querying the OS font
   system (`fc-match`, `/System/Library/Fonts`, `C:\Windows\Fonts`, `fontconfig`, etc.) — there is
   no rendering code yet in Phase 0, but the constraint is enforced now via a static grep test so
   it can never be introduced silently later.
4. **A machine-readable font manifest** (`src/anima/assets/fonts/manifest.json`) listing, per
   bundled font: `pack`, `role`, `family`, `weight`, `style`, `filename`, `license_spdx`, and
   `source_url`. This is the data this task hands off to the Phase 3 `font-bundling`-consuming task
   (style-pack schema implementation, which maps `StylePack.fonts` roles → these bundled files per
   `data-model.md` § style pack fields) — that later task reads this manifest rather than
   re-researching typography.
5. **An attribution file shipped in-package** (`src/anima/assets/fonts/ATTRIBUTION.md`), listing
   every bundled font's family/weight, license (name + SPDX id), copyright holder, and source URL,
   generated to stay in lockstep with the manifest (every manifest entry must appear in the
   attribution file; a test enforces this so the two can't silently drift). This is what makes the
   `non-functional-requirements.md` compliance bullet ("attribution files shipped in-package") true
   — the file must be retrievable from the *installed* package via `importlib.resources`, not just
   sitting in the repo.
6. **Font-validity checks.** Each bundled file is opened with `fontTools.ttLib` (added as a
   dev-only test dependency, not a runtime dependency — actual glyph rendering at render time goes
   through the pinned skia-python/FreeType path once the renderer exists in Phase 1) and asserted
   to parse cleanly and cover the ASCII printable range plus common narration punctuation (curly
   quotes, em/en dash, ellipsis) that beat-narration text and subtitles will need. This catches a
   corrupt download or an incomplete character set before any scene code ever tries to render text
   with it.

## Explicitly out of scope for this task

- The `StylePack` schema / loader code that maps pack roles to these bundled files at runtime —
  that is `data-model.md`'s style-pack schema, which ships in Phase 3 ("Style-pack schema
  implemented ... with pack-version pinning"). This task produces the vetted, bundled *assets and
  manifest* Phase 3 will consume; it does not write the `StylePack` Python class.
- Actual subtitle rendering or `Text` node glyph rasterization (Phase 1/2) and procedural
  glyph-tracing paint-stroke text (Phase 3 capability 4, "procedural text stroking that traces
  glyph outlines properly").
- The full typography breadth each pack needs at pack-parity ("typography" is listed again in the
  Phase 3 `crude` done-when and the Phase 5 `flat` done-when bullets) — this task's bar is the
  minimum viable `display` + `body` role coverage per pack with clean licensing; expanding roles
  (bold/italic variants, a numeric/label role, condensed variants for tight map labels) can happen
  later without redoing the license-vetting infrastructure.
- Golden-image tests of actual rendered text (no renderer exists yet in Phase 0) — the validity
  check here is at the font-file level (parses, has needed glyphs), not the pixel level.

## Acceptance shape

Done when: a font manifest exists naming license-vetted, permissively-licensed fonts covering at
least a `display` and `body` role for both `crude` and `flat`; the actual font binaries are bundled
under `src/anima/assets/fonts/<pack>/` and demonstrably ship inside the built wheel (not merely
present in the repo checkout); no source file resolves fonts from the OS; an in-package attribution
file lists every bundled font's license and source, verifiably in sync with the manifest and
retrievable via `importlib.resources` from the installed package; and every bundled font file
parses cleanly with full coverage of the character set beat-narration text will need.

deliverables.description: Machine-readable font manifest (src/anima/assets/fonts/manifest.json) naming license-vetted typography candidates for both packs: at least a `display` and a `body` role for `crude` (informal/hand-lettered feel) and for `flat` (geometric sans), each entry carrying family, weight, style, filename, license_spdx, and source_url, with every license_spdx restricted to a permissive allow-list (OFL-1.1, Apache-2.0, MIT, CC0-1.0, Unlicense).

Font binaries actually bundled as package data under src/anima/assets/fonts/crude/ and src/anima/assets/fonts/flat/, wired into pyproject.toml's package-data configuration so a built wheel contains every font file the manifest references, with no source file in src/anima resolving fonts from the OS (no fc-match/fontconfig/system font directory references).

verification.command: rm -rf dist_font_check && python -m build --wheel --outdir dist_font_check && python -c "import zipfile, glob, json; manifest = json.load(open('src/anima/assets/fonts/manifest.json')); wheel = zipfile.ZipFile(glob.glob('dist_font_check/*.whl')[0]); names = set(wheel.namelist()); missing = [e['filename'] for e in manifest if not any(n.endswith(e['filename']) for n in names)]; assert not missing, f'fonts missing from wheel: {missing}'; print('all manifest fonts present in wheel:', len(manifest))" && ! grep -RIn --include=*.py -E "(fc-match|fontconfig|/System/Library/Fonts|Windows\\\\Fonts)" src/anima

deliverables.description: Every bundled font file is a valid, parseable font (via fontTools.ttLib, added as a dev-only test dependency) with glyph coverage for the ASCII printable range plus common narration punctuation (curly quotes, em/en dash, ellipsis) needed by beat narration and subtitle text.

In-package attribution file (src/anima/assets/fonts/ATTRIBUTION.md) listing every bundled font's family/weight, license name + SPDX id, copyright holder, and source URL, verified programmatically to be in sync with the manifest (no manifest entry missing from attribution) and retrievable from the installed package via importlib.resources rather than only present in the repo checkout.
